deprescribing_recommendation: Review renal function, reassess ongoing need, reduce dose if renal impairment exists, and avoid unnecessary combination with CNS depressants.
Use cautiously in elderly with regular renal function and bleeding risk assessment; review dose appropriateness and avoid unnecessary prolonged therapy.

Reassess pain indication and opioid burden; prioritize safer analgesics where appropriate, avoid duplicate acetaminophen exposure, taper gradually if chronic use, and monitor sedation and liver function.
Avoid prolonged use in older adults when possible; reassess pain control, taper gradually to prevent withdrawal, monitor for CNS adverse effects and drug interactions.
Avoid routine use in elderly; consider gradual taper. Prefer non-pharmacologic anxiety management or safer alternatives depending on indication.
Reassess chronic need regularly; avoid long-term use where possible, monitor renal function and blood pressure, and use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.
Evaluate ongoing opioid need and total acetaminophen exposure; use safer pain management options when possible, taper gradually, and monitor sedation, liver function, and pain control.
Reassess need, especially if used off-label for insomnia/anxiety. Gradual taper recommended. Keep lowest effective dose if psychosis/Parkinson�s psychosis indication exists.

Avoid long-term use in older adults; gradually taper if chronic use, prioritize nonpharmacologic sleep interventions (sleep hygiene/CBT-I), reassess ongoing need, and avoid combination with other sedatives where possible.
Reassess chronic use; taper gradually if discontinuing. Use only when benefit outweighs risk (e.g., seizure disorders).
Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration; reassess pain control regularly, consider non-opioid alternatives, taper slowly if long-term therapy, and monitor renal function and sedation.
Use cautiously; individualize dose, monitor glucose closely, and simplify regimen if recurrent hypoglycemia occurs.
Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration; reassess need regularly.
Use cautiously with periodic reassessment of bleeding risk, renal/hepatic function, and treatment duration; avoid inappropriate long-term use.

Reassess necessity and glycemic goals in older adults; consider switching to lower hypoglycemia-risk alternatives when appropriate, reduce dose if recurrent hypoglycemia occurs, and monitor blood glucose closely.
Dose adjustment based on renal function; reassess benefit vs sedation

Avoid chronic systemic use in older adults, especially in cardiovascular disease or CKD; consider topical diclofenac for localized pain, reassess ongoing need, and use lowest effective dose if necessary.
Monitor INR closely, review interacting medications, assess bleeding risk, and consider DOAC alternatives when clinically appropriate.

Avoid long-term use in older adults unless clearly indicated; use lowest effective dose for shortest duration, assess renal/GI/cardiovascular risk, consider gastroprotection, and prefer safer alternatives (e.g., acetaminophen or topical therapy where appropriate).
Consider safer bladder alternatives; reassess anticholinergic burden
